observations: ct-textfield renders a bare <input type="text">; it carries no images or icons of its own.

standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: ct-textfield renders only the <input> itself — no label, no <fieldset>/<legend>. Its programmatic name/relationship depends entirely on the implementer pairing it with an external label (a `ct-label for=""`/`<label for="">` whose `for` matches this component's `id`) or, absent a visible label, setting `aria-label` directly. Neither is something the component can enforce on itself.

ct-textfield has no `autocomplete` prop (matching upstream `textfield.component.yml`, which doesn't expose one either). Compliance for fields collecting common user data (name, email, etc.) depends on the implementer adding a raw `autocomplete` attribute via the host element, or CivicTheme's own guidance elsewhere in the surrounding form markup — this component alone can't infer the field's purpose from `name` or `placeholder`.

The `invalid` state changes border-color/background-color (and sets `aria-invalid="true"` programmatically) but adds no icon or text of its own. A sighted user relying on color alone to notice an invalid field is a real risk unless the implementer pairs this component with `ct-field-message` (which does add a status icon + text) to surface the same state non-visually-only.

Text color (`--ct-textfield-light-color` #33444a on `--ct-textfield-light-background-color` #fdfdfd; `--ct-textfield-dark-color` #e7f9ff on `--ct-textfield-dark-background-color` #002937) and border colors in every state (default, hover, focus, invalid) resolve to real CivicTheme token values that comfortably clear 4.5:1 in both themes.
Field text and placeholder are set in rem units (`--ct-typography-label-small-*`); text remains visible and readable at 200% zoom.
The field renders real text (value/placeholder), never an image of text.
The field is `width: 100%` of its container with `box-sizing: border-box`; it reflows with its parent rather than causing horizontal scrolling.

The default border (`--ct-textfield-light-border-color` #5c5d5e / `--ct-textfield-dark-border-color` #a6bac1) and the focus-visible outline (`--ct-textfield-light-outline-color`/`--ct-textfield-dark-outline-color` #8b5cd7) each meet the 3:1 minimum against their adjacent background.
Text is visible/readable when the line spacing is at least 1.5 times the font size, letter spacing is at least 0.12 times the font size, and word spacing is at least 0.16 times the font size.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)
observations: ct-textfield triggers no supplementary content (tooltip, popover) on hover or focus.
A native <input type="text"> is fully operable via keyboard alone (focus, type, select, clear) with no custom key handling to interfere with it.
Standard tab/shift-tab moves focus into and out of the field; nothing traps keyboard focus.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: The field is a single native focusable element; its position in the surrounding form's focus order is inherited from DOM order, which the implementer controls.
ct-textfield never renders a link.

ct-textfield renders no heading or label text of its own — that responsibility belongs to the paired `ct-label`/`<label>` (see 1.3.1). There is no `label`/`legend` element inside this component's own shadow DOM for the mechanical text-quality heuristic to evaluate.
`:focus-visible` sets a solid outline using `--ct-textfield-<theme>-outline-color` at `--ct-textfield-outline-width`/`-outline-offset`, in both themes.
The component adds no overlay or sticky content of its own that could obscure the field when it receives focus.

If the implementer sets `aria-label` while a visible label also exists (rather than relying on native `for`/`id` association alone), that `aria-label` text must still contain the visible label's text verbatim. Simplest compliant approach, same as `ct-label`'s own guidance: let `for`/`id` supply the accessible name and skip `aria-label` whenever a visible label is present.

The field's fixed padding (0.8125rem vertical, 1rem horizontal) plus its line-height give it a rendered height around 46px regardless of variant, and it defaults to `width: 100%` of its container — comfortably above the 24x24 CSS pixel minimum in ordinary use.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting the correct lang attribute on the html element.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting lang attributes on parts of the page that differ from the main language.
Focusing the field triggers no context change of its own — no navigation, no submission, no unexpected DOM change.
Typing into the field triggers no context change of its own; the component does not auto-submit or navigate on input.

Setting `invalid` sets `aria-invalid="true"` and applies invalid styling, but ct-textfield renders no error text of its own. Compliance depends on the implementer pairing it with `ct-field-message` (or equivalent) to describe *what* is invalid, not just *that* it is.
Same dependency as 1.3.1/2.4.6: the field itself provides no visible instructions or label text — that's `ct-label`'s (or the implementer's) responsibility to supply and associate.
Compliance depends on the implementer providing helpful suggestions when errors are detected, typically via a paired `ct-field-message`.

The native <input type="text"> exposes the `textbox` role and its value/required/disabled/aria-invalid state automatically. The accessible *name*, however, is only present when the implementer either pairs a `for`/`id`-matched label or sets `aria-label` directly — with neither, the field has no accessible name at all (axe's `label`/`aria-input-field-name` rule would flag it).

`aria-invalid` toggling is programmatic but not itself an `aria-live` announcement; compliance depends on the implementer exposing a validation-error status message (e.g. via `ct-field-message` with an appropriate live region) so assistive technology users are notified without having to re-focus the field.
